findings.reachability: Admin-only (rendered inside Settings page callback, requires manage_options to reach the settings page). Arguments to add_query_arg are hard-coded, so no attacker-controlled data flows into the URL in this file; however the pattern is exploitable in similar code.
findings.justification: add_query_arg() output is echoed directly into an href attribute without esc_url(), violating the WordPress security standard that every URL in HTML context must be wrapped in esc_url(); although arguments are hard-coded here, get_home_url() is filterable and the pattern is a canonical source of XSS in WordPress plugins when callers pass $_SERVER or $_GET values.

detection_pattern.description: Finds echo/print or href assignments where add_query_arg result is used without esc_url(); this is the most common XSS pattern in WordPress plugins because add_query_arg may propagate attacker-controlled REQUEST_URI when called without explicit $url.

findings.reachability: Admin-only (guarded by screen_id check on line 330). Exploitable only if attacker can supply or modify a translation (.mo/.po) file for the block-bad-queries text domain, e.g. via file-write access, malicious translation plugin, or a compromised supply chain.
findings.justification: _e() outputs translated strings directly into bare JavaScript string literals without esc_js() or wp_json_encode(); a translation value containing a single-quote or back-slash can break out of the JS string and execute arbitrary JavaScript in any admin's browser.

detection_pattern.section_name: XSS: _e() or __() inside JavaScript string literal
detection_pattern.description: Finds translation function calls (_e/__) used directly inside <script> blocks; without esc_js() or wp_json_encode() wrapping, a translation value with special characters breaks JS string context and enables XSS.

findings.reachability: Any logged-in user who can load any admin_init page and who obtains a valid nonce for action 'bbq_dismiss_notice'. The capability check at line 412 fires only after nonce passes, meaning nonce is evaluated for all logged-in users regardless of role.
findings.justification: The capability check (current_user_can) is placed inside the nonce-verified branch rather than before it; WordPress best practice is to check capabilities first and then verify the nonce, because verifying a nonce for a low-privilege user wastes server side nonce table state and leaks timing information; in this case the state-mutation (update_option) is correctly gated by the inner capability check so practical impact is negligible, but the ordering is a defensive weakness.

detection_pattern.section_name: Auth: capability check after nonce verification
detection_pattern.description: Flags handlers where current_user_can appears inside the block opened by wp_verify_nonce rather than before it; correct order is capability check first to fail-fast before spending nonce verification resources.

findings.reachability: Admin-only (gated by nonce + manage_options). Redirect target is built via admin_url() which is safe, so not exploitable here; risk materialises if redirect target is later changed to incorporate user input.
findings.justification: wp_redirect() is used instead of wp_safe_redirect(); while the destination is constructed from admin_url() and is not user-controlled, using wp_redirect() (which allows off-site redirects) instead of wp_safe_redirect() (which restricts to same-site) violates the principle of using the safest available API; if the location construction is later modified to include user input the redirect becomes exploitable.

detection_pattern.section_name: Open Redirect: wp_redirect instead of wp_safe_redirect
detection_pattern.description: Flags use of wp_redirect() in plugin code; wp_safe_redirect() should be preferred for admin-side redirects because it restricts destinations to the same host, preventing open redirect vulnerabilities if the location argument ever incorporates attacker-controlled data.
